title: Hidden Necrosis Risk: 3 Tight Areas Filler Can Cause Capillary Compression [Aesthetics Mastery Show]
filename: Hidden Necrosis Risk： 3 Tight Areas Filler Can Cause Capillary Compression [Aesthetics Mastery Show].en.vtt
excerpt: I feel like chatty's here but just useless he's kind of present like you kind of can talk to him but he just doesn't do anything apart from amazing editing welcome to the aesthetics mastery show i'm dr tim pierce hi i'm miranda pearce and today we are talking about an unusual cau…

segments.text: i feel like chatty's here but just useless he's kind of present like you kind of can talk to him but he just doesn't do anything apart from amazing editing welcome to the aesthetics mastery show i'm dr tim pierce hi i'm miranda pearce and today we are talking about an unusual cause for necrosis this is capillary compression it's unspoken about many places not a lot of evidence for it but i hope you'll follow my logic and believe that it is possible okay it sounds scary what's going on with this so this comes from the theory in fact it's well established in medicine a pressure saw if you put pressure on something for long enough you don't get blood supply to that area and the skin dies that's essentially what anyone who's bed bound gets after around between two and eight hours of resting on a particular point you get necrosis of that skin

and i think the same thing can happen with dermal filler when placed in certain parts of the face in particular okay so where are we talking so number one area i think is the glabella and this happens when treating frown lines or in the forehead as well and what you'll see in these cases i believe is a different shape to the necrotic lesion so you don't see the path of the artery now in many lesions hopefully we'll be able in many lesions hopefully we'll be able to show you one now from a paper where you can see the actual path of the artery that's been included and that's very clear that there's filler in the blood vessel but there are other types of injury that i've seen where it looks much rounder it's not clearly following the path of a vessel and it tends to be in an area where you're where there's little room effectively to add volume and that makes it more likely that pressure is causing the necrosis i believe okay and where else apart from glabella so after the bella there's the nose tip i think it's that's a fairly common place to get sort of brewing necrosis often it's just redness and pain people get and then i suspect also the

chin i've seen a couple of lesions in the chin that look suspicious to me as pressure lesions rather than blocked arteries okay so tell us a bit more about this roundness like how would we differ how would we differentiate it from what we've been used to seeing well it's really to do with the fact that if you block a blood vessel they are literally like tree trunks they're like they're they branch out and you tend to see it following a line of where the vessel is so if you block the superchocular artery you often get um an area of necrosis that follows that artery into the forehead you can see it if you block a superlabial artery you get necrosis that follows up the lip and often even leads right to the tip of the nose these are all indications that it's following the vasculature and blocking the capillaries that leave from that vasculature tracking yeah along the vessel so if you know your anatomy it becomes really clear you can see it in lips i've certainly seen an inferior labial artery occlusion that you could see tracking down heading back towards the facial artery so you could see the area on the lip and then

the palate that followed it so that there's a sense of it following the anatomy and some occlusions just don't follow that at all they just seem to have a an area of roundness as the best way of describing it it's a focal point where there's necrosis that's a bit different to a linear a line of necrosis following a blood vessel so how does pressure cause an occlusion well in order for blood to bring oxygen to tissue obviously if it's not able to flow into that tissue you're not going to get enough oxygen and the tissue will eventually die so anything that you inject in fact you can actually simulate this with saline if you inject a bolus of saline into some of the skin you will see the skin go white and it can stay that way for three four seconds before the saline basically seeps away and the pressure of the blood will then overwhelm that tissue pressure and blood will keep flowing it's essentially the battle between pressure in the tissue and your and the pressure in the capillaries which comes from your blood pressure so if you the higher you put the pressure in the tissue the higher the blood pressure would be in order to keep putting

oxygen through that tissue in the blood that it's carrying so all it takes is an area where the pro the pressure is sustained for long enough that no blood gets through for a long enough period that those two those cells die from a lack of oxygen but just to be clear then so you're not seeing an eye on a blood vessel that's right you're near it you may not even be near it you're basically putting pressure into the around the capillaries in that area to such a level that no blood flows through those capillaries you can do this just by pushing if i if you push on your lip for five seconds and release it you'll see it's pale when you release it that's because you squeezed all the blood out of those capillaries if you were to hold that area continuously you would get necrosis because no oxygen gets through while you're keeping the blood out so it's you can cause it's basically a pressure saw as i said at the right beginning pressure stops the blood flowing and if you do that for long enough you get necrosis and it's as simple as that so some areas of the skin just for one reason or another seem to sustain pressure better there's

something about the skin quality that's different in the forehead tip of the nose maybe and i think sometimes the chin i think for example if you think of your cheek there's loads of room it feels like there's loads of potential there so maybe superficially with high volumes you could do it but in normal injections you're not going to cause pressure whereas that seems to be different in the forehead the nose and the chin it makes into the nose isn't it because there's not much space for anything going on there yeah the potential space is less and it's different with different people some people it's quite adhered i think it's a little bit more risky if you've had uh surgical rhinoplasty there's much more scarring and the tissue is much more stuck down in fact it's one of the reasons you might stop a procedure i've done that a couple of times when you you're trying to inject and as you inject you just see the filler makes the skin blanch and it doesn't separate from the periosteum at all so the skin in the and the bone are almost stuck together and you get this little bubble of white if you were to leave that it might cause necrosis so you would then massage it until it was completely gone and that's caused a scar tissue in noses the ones i've seen have been due to

non-surgical rhinoplasty okay which is a shame because often they go wrong don't then we we're clearing up the mess yeah well it's one of the most common reasons to do a non-surgical rhinoplasty is fixing a surgical rhinoplasty but what about the chin it seems like it's quite a lot of space there yeah i think there is in most people but there are a percentage of patients when you're injecting them that this they just feel the anatomy feels a bit tighter and i'm i've seen two cases that were a bit like this and this is the one i'm least sure about but it does seem to me i've had a few cases where i'm injecting and it just feels firm so as you inject you see blanching it doesn't feel like there's loads of room and those patients i think are a bit more likely to cause a problem and the other thing with chin augmentation is you're often using quite high volumes and very stiff products so the combination of using three or four mils in an area with a very with the stiffest product you can get basically to emulate bone um in maybe a patient's anatomy where there's just a bit less room i think you can cause that okay and it you say that it is red is

that's one way it manifests what other signs can you say that you've achieved this bedsaw situation um well it's often you well redness i think is the body's attempt to cut accommodating for it so if you think about what happens if you were to decrease the blood supply somewhere you get a buildup of cellular metabolites that nearly all of them cause vasodilation they cause an inflammatory response in a way to try and compensate if you've got it if you're on the right side and it can compensate all you get is redness i've seen this in non-surgical rhinoplasty where someone has a little bit of discomfort on the day of the procedure it's a little bit painful and then it just stays red for a long time and then you reverse it and it goes away that i think might be caused by um a compensatory mechanism for the higher pressure so are there any summary takeaways for how we can diagnose and avoid this pressure sore effect well the thing i think most people are going to be thinking is how do you even know this is

happening because it's incredibly hard to actually you we don't tend to scan our patients and see where the filler is you've got a lesion there you can tell that they're struggling and you tend to reverse it so we don't even know for sure what percentage of people doing emergency reversals are reversing hematoma and we don't know if they're in the vessel or if they're just in the tissue causing a pressure effect so that that's the area that might make people less certain what i'm hoping you'll get from this episode is knowing that there might be another reason why you can get necrosis which might explain an atypical presentation because we're we need to get this diagnosis of potential necrosis right while there's time to reverse it and if you're only thinking about blocked arteries there are occasions where you might look at this picture and think you know what that's very unlikely because the archery doesn't run there or it's not following the root of the artery and if you at least know that this might be a potential cause it might possibly prevent a scar on someone of course we don't know for sure where this is but what you will discover

with time is that if you reverse a procedure like this and it immediately looks better you're going to be more and more confident that there is something around the pressure that can cause this i think this fundamental science is sound if you can get a bed saw from pressure you can get a pressure saw from dermal filler too for me that makes perfect sense how often how frequent how severe it is very hard to know but i hope that anyone watching this will jump to having that on their list of differentials when they make the decision whether or not to reverse and not only think about the pattern of occlusion that you might see a bit more frequently than this cause and hopefully it will help people make a better decision but also when you're assessing where it's appropriate to fill in the first place now that you know that not to go for the tighter areas yeah so that would be the other thing is in when designing treatments just bear in mind certain areas a bit more likely to cause a pressure effect and that could be a risk for some of your patients so yeah you should design slightly safer treatment plans if you understand this

as a potential risk so i hope you found that useful remember you can download a summary of everything we talked about in the last three episodes it's a simple guide with all the arteries that we've talked about how to inject a bit more safely so hope that helps you see you next week take care you
